1: Oh, that you would tear open the heavens and come down — that the mountains would quake at your presence. The heaven-rending theophany request is the lament's climactic petition. In ANE cosmology, the heavens were a solid dome (raqia) separating the divine realm from the human; the request for YHWH to tear (qara) it open asks for a violent rupture of the normal cosmic boundary. Mountains quaking at the divine presence is the standard Sinai theophany vocabulary (Exodus 19:16-18; Psalms 114:4-7) — the exilic community wants Sinai-level divine intervention.
2: As when fire kindles brushwood and water boils — to make your name known to your enemies, so that the nations would tremble at your presence. The Sinai theophany (Exodus 19:18: YHWH descended in fire; the smoke rose like smoke from a furnace) is the model. The nations trembling at the divine name repeats the Reed-Sea effect: at the Exodus, the nations heard and trembled (Exodus 15:14-16). The petition asks for a repetition of name-making-by-theophany: demonstrate your power dramatically so that the nations fear you as at the Exodus.
3: When you did awe-inspiring things we did not expect, you came down — the mountains quaked at your presence. The historical precedent cited is the Sinai event and the wilderness theophanies. The phrase we did not expect (lo niqaveh) captures the quality of divine action that exceeds human anticipation: YHWH acted beyond what the community had calculated or hoped for. The appeal is: do the unexpected thing again — the thing no one dares to ask for specifically because it seems too large.
4: From ancient times no one has heard, no ear has perceived, no eye has seen any God besides you who acts on behalf of those who wait for him. The uniqueness of YHWH as a God who acts for his waiting people distinguishes him from the ANE gods who required human action and ritual manipulation to secure their favor. YHWH's initiative-for-waiters is his distinctive covenantal character. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians 2:9 (applied to the hidden wisdom now revealed by the Spirit) — but he applies it to what YHWH has prepared, extending the future horizon of the promise.
5: You come to the aid of those who joyfully do right, those who remember you in your ways. But you were angry, and we sinned — in our sins we have long continued, and how can we be saved? The transition from divine character to communal confession mirrors the structure of the Psalms of lament. The community acknowledges that YHWH supports the righteous but admits they are not that community: they sinned and continued in sin across generations. The rhetorical question — how can we be saved? — is not despair but the opening for divine answer: only by your unilateral action, since we have no merit to appeal to.
6: We have all become like one who is unclean — all our righteous deeds are like a filthy rag; we all wither like a leaf and our iniquities, like the wind, sweep us away. The filthy rag (begad iddim) is the menstrual cloth — the most extreme category of ritual impurity in Leviticus 15. Even the community's best religious performances are categorized at the most impure end of the purity-scale. The withering-leaf image captures transience and vulnerability: the wind sweeps both the autumn leaf and the community's sinful members away without resistance.
7: There is no one who calls on your name, who stirs himself to take hold of you — for you have hidden your face from us and given us over to the power of our iniquities. The absence of effective intercession (ein qore be-shimkha) is both diagnosis and the occasion for the prayer being offered: the community is praying precisely because no one has been praying. The hidden face (cf. Isaiah 59:2) has been given a specific mechanism — YHWH gives them over (va-temugenu) to the power of their own iniquities, a self-reinforcing exile from the covenant relationship.
8: But now, LORD, you are our Father — we are the clay and you are our potter; we are all the work of your hands. The potter-clay image (drawn from Jeremiah 18:1-12) shifts the appeal from Israel's merit to YHWH's creative ownership: the clay has no rights before the potter, but the potter has invested himself in the clay's formation. The atah avinu (you are our Father) repeats the appeal of Isaiah 63:16 — the Father-Potter relationship is the only ground of appeal when covenant merit is exhausted. Paul develops the potter-clay sovereignty in Romans 9:20-21.
9: Do not be exceedingly angry, LORD — do not remember iniquity forever. Please look — we are all your people. The three-part petition: limit your anger, forget the iniquity, see us as yours. The appeal to YHWH's self-limitation of anger echoes Isaiah 40:1-2 (the comfort that speaks that Jerusalem's warfare is over, her iniquity paid). We are all your people is the covenant-identity claim made without merit: not we have behaved as your people but we are, by election, your people — the relationship predates and survives the failure.
10: Your holy cities have become a wasteland — Zion is a wilderness, Jerusalem a desolation. The lament catalogs the physical evidence of YHWH's absence: the holy cities (plural — all the Judean towns, not just Jerusalem) have become the midbar (wilderness/wasteland) that Israel was meant to have left at the Exodus. Zion and Jerusalem named specifically as wilderness and desolation are the visible proof of covenant disruption — the holy city has reverted to pre-creation formlessness.
11: Our holy and glorious house where our fathers praised you has been burned with fire, and all our cherished places have become desolate. The temple's destruction by Babylonian fire (2 Kings 25:9) is the specific catastrophe the entire lament has been approaching. Our cherished places (machmadesenu) — the word from the root of the covenant's most desirable things — have become desolate. The temple was not merely a religious building but the intersection of heaven and earth, the cosmic axis of the covenant community's existence. Its burning is the most extreme form of the covenant's disruption.
12: Will you hold yourself back for all this, LORD? Will you keep silent while you afflict us so grievously? The lament's closing challenge-petition is the sharpest form of covenant prayer: given all this evidence of catastrophe, will YHWH continue to restrain himself and stay silent? The holding-back (titapaq) and silence (teheshah) are addressed directly to YHWH as choices he is making. The lament does not accept divine inaction as inevitable — it names it as a choice YHWH can change, and petitions for that change. The question hangs unanswered in the text, creating the expectation that chapter 65 will provide the divine response.
